summary: Gruber systematically dismantles the credibility of analyst Ashok Kumar, whose research note about a 7-inch iPad was being cited by Om Malik and others. He catalogs Kumar's history of wrong predictions about Apple products: claiming the iPad would be carrier-subsidized, that it would use Qualcomm/Verizon, that iPad demand was weak, that iPhone sales in China were disappointing (before it even went on sale), and that the original iPhone was a dud. Gruber argues that relying on Kumar's predictions is essentially worthless.
key_points: Kumar predicted the iPad would be carrier-subsidized through Verizon with a Qualcomm chip — it launched as a GSM device on AT&T with no subsidies
Kumar claimed iPad production exceeded demand, when in reality supply couldn't meet demand for months
Kumar reported disappointing iPhone sales through China Unicom three days before the phone actually went on sale there
Kumar declared the original iPhone a 'dud' one month after launch, comparing it to a Harry Potter squib
The broader point is that tech media uncritically amplifies analyst predictions regardless of track record
money_quotes: Betting on a 7-inch iPad based on a Kumar "research note" is pretty much like betting on the time of day based on a stopped clock.
The iPhone did not go on sale through China Unicom until 15 October 2009.
In fact, the supply of iPads was unable to meet demand until just a few weeks ago. The iPad is selling faster in its first year than the iPhone did.
Will Apple make a 7-inch iPad? And if so, would it be the new size for the iPad, or a second size, alongside the existing just-under-10-inch size? I don't know.
Apple had told manufacturers it wanted to produce a million iPads per month and 'that's clearly in excess of demand,' said Ashok Kumar.
key_insight: Tech media routinely amplifies analyst predictions without checking track records, allowing consistently wrong analysts to maintain influence through sheer repetition.
